attributes.stem:
A 19-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-week history of a lump in her right breast. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Breast examination reveals a smooth, well-circumscribed lump in the right breast, approximately 3 cm in diameter. The lump is mobile. An ultrasound reveals a 3 cm mass with well-defined borders but a heterogeneous echotexture.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Referral for mammography to better characterise the lesion
Refer to Breast Screen for further assessment
Reassurance and encouragement of monthly breast checks
Referral for MRI to better characterise the lesion
attributes.explanation:
Referral to a breast surgeon is the most appropriate next step in management. While the history and examination findings are consistent with a fibroadenoma (mobile, smooth, well-circumscribed mass in a young woman), the heterogeneous echotexture on ultrasound is not typical and should be treated as suspicious. A typical fibroadenoma appears homogeneous on ultrasound. The discordance between the clinical and radiological findings necessitates specialist assessment. A breast surgeon can determine the need for any further investigations, including possible biopsy, and appropriate follow-up.

Referral for mammography is not appropriate in this case. Mammography generally yields indeterminate results in women under 35 years of age due to increased breast density. The radiation exposure, while small, should also be avoided in young women when possible. Mammography is unlikely to change the management in this scenario as ultrasound has already identified concerning features.

Refer to Breast Screen for further assessment is not appropriate. Breast Screen programs are typically designed for asymptomatic women over 40 years old for routine screening, not for the assessment of symptomatic breast lumps in young women. These services are not equipped to manage symptomatic breast disease, which requires different protocols and more urgent assessment.

Reassurance and encouragement of monthly breast checks would be inadequate management given the atypical ultrasound findings. While breast self-examination is important for early detection of changes, it should not replace proper investigation of a suspicious lump, particularly one with concerning imaging features. This approach could lead to delayed diagnosis if the mass proves to be significant.

Referral for MRI to better characterise the lesion is not the most appropriate next step. While MRI can provide detailed images of breast tissue, it is not typically used as a first-line investigation for breast lumps, especially in young women. MRI is usually reserved for specific indications such as screening high-risk patients, evaluating response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, or investigating particular clinical scenarios as determined by a specialist.

In general, new breast lumps should be investigated using the 'triple test': clinical history and breast examination, imaging (mammography and/or ultrasound), and non-excisional biopsy (either by fine needle aspiration or core biopsy). However, this approach needs to be tailored to the patient's characteristics. In young women, ultrasound is the preferred initial imaging modality.

While breast cancer is extremely uncommon in teenagers, other diagnoses such as phyllodes tumour should be considered when imaging findings are inconsistent with the clinical picture. The breast surgeon may recommend core biopsy to exclude phyllodes tumour or other pathology, or might opt for interval ultrasound in about six months to ensure no significant increase in size. Any change in size might indicate a phyllodes tumour, which would then require surgical excision.
learningPoints: When imaging of a breast lump is inconsistent with history or examination, refer to a breast surgeon.
In young women, breast lumps are best investigated with ultrasound.
Heterogeneous echotexture on ultrasound of a breast mass warrants specialist assessment even if clinical features suggest benign disease.
references: Investigation of a new breast symptom: a guide for General Practitioners [Internet]. Cancer Australia. Australian Government; 2021 [cited 2023Apr28]. Available from: https://www.canceraustralia.gov.au/inbsguide
